name: Sisapo from Fuencaliente – circular by Torrecampo and Azuel – Parque Natural Valle de Alcudia y Sierra Madrona
name_original: Sisapo desde Fuencaliente – circular por Torrecampo y Azuel – Parque Natural Valle de Alcudia y Sierra Madrona
region: Valle De Alcudia Y Sierra Madrona
region_intro: Road cycling in Valle de Alcudia y Sierra Madrona offers diverse terrain across Castilla-La Mancha. The region features two distinct landscapes: the expansive plains of Valle de Alcudia, characterized by ancient oak trees and pastures, and the rugged, mountainous Sierra Madrona to the south with its ridges and unique rock formations. Rivers and streams flow through these areas, contributing to the scenery. Many routes feature well-paved surfaces, making the area suitable for road cyclists. Best road cycling routes in Valle De Alcudia Y Sierra Madrona The most popular road cycling route is Puerto Madrona y Puerto de los Rehoyos — circular por el Pantano del Montoro, a 68.4 miles (110.0 km) trail that takes 6 hours 4 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gains and passes by the Montoro Reservoir. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Sisapo from Fuencaliente – circular by Torrecampo and Azuel – Parque Natural Valle de Alcudia y Sierra Madrona, a difficult 79.8 miles (128.5 km) path. This route explores areas around Fuencaliente and the natural park, offering varied terrain. Local road cyclists also love the Puerto de los Rehoyos – Puerto Madrona Spring loop from Mestanza, a 40.5 miles (65.3 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain, often completed in about 3 hours 53 minutes. Road cycling in Valle De Alcudia Y Sierra Madrona is defined by expansive plains, rugged mountains, and ancient oak trees. The network offers options for different ability levels, including 3 moderate and 9 difficult routes. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 200 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Valle De Alcudia Y Sierra Madrona's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Valle de Alcudia y Sierra Madrona?
A: The region offers a diverse landscape for road cycling. You'll find expansive plains with ancient oak trees and pastures in the Valle de Alcudia, contrasting with the rugged, mountainous Sierra Madrona to the south, featuring ridges and unique rock formations. Many routes boast well-paved surfaces, making them suitable for road cyclists.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Valle de Alcudia y Sierra Madrona?
A: While the region is known for its challenging routes, with 9 out of 12 routes rated difficult, there are 3 moderate routes available. These offer less elevation and shorter durations, providing options for those with good fitness but seeking a less strenuous ride. For families, it's advisable to check individual route profiles for specific suitability, as many routes involve significant distances and elevation.

Q: What are some notable natural attractions I might see along road cycling routes?
A: The region is rich in natural beauty. Cyclists can encounter natural waterfalls like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4342897" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Batanera Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6470149" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Chorrera de Ventillas Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, as well as seasonal wetlands and prominent quartzite rock formations. The routes often pass through areas with ancient oak trees and diverse vegetation, contributing to the scenic experience.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural landmarks accessible by road bike?
A: Yes, the region has a rich cultural heritage. You can find sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3169663" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peñaescrita Cave Paintings&lt;/a&gt;, which feature ancient schematic art. Some routes might also pass near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3069525" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Batanera Rock Art Site&lt;/a&gt;, offering a glimpse into the area's past.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes in Valle de Alcudia y Sierra Madrona?
A: Yes, the region offers several circular road cycling routes. A popular option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1249475549/puerto-madrona-y-puerto-de-los-rehoyos-circular-por-el-pantano-del-montoro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto Madrona y Puerto de los Rehoyos — circular por el Pantano del Montoro&lt;/a&gt;, which is a challenging 110 km loop passing by the Montoro Reservoir. Another is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1070290629/sisapo-from-fuencaliente-circular-by-torrecampo-and-azuel-parque-natural-valle-de-alcudia-y-sierra-madrona" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sisapo from Fuencaliente – circular by Torrecampo and Azuel&lt;/a&gt;, exploring areas around Fuencaliente.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Valle de Alcudia y Sierra Madrona?
A: The region experiences varied weather. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the colder, potentially wetter conditions of winter. These seasons provide comfortable riding conditions and showcase the natural beauty of the landscape.

Q: Are there mountain passes to challenge road cyclists in the area?
A: Absolutely. The Sierra Madrona part of the region features rugged mountainous terrain with significant elevation gains. You can find challenging mountain passes such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1018077" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto Madrona&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7910077" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Niefla&lt;/a&gt;, which are popular among experienced road cyclists looking for a demanding ride.

Q: Where can I find parking for road cycling routes in Valle de Alcudia y Sierra Madrona?
A: Specific parking information varies by route starting point. Many towns and villages within the region, such as Fuencaliente or Mestanza, serve as common starting points for routes and typically offer public parking facilities. It's recommended to check the details for your chosen route's trailhead for the most accurate parking information.

Q: Are there any regulations or permits required for cycling in the Natural Park of Valle de Alcudia y Sierra Madrona?
A: The Natural Park of Valle de Alcudia y Sierra Madrona is committed to ecotourism. While general road cycling on public roads typically doesn't require specific permits, it's always advisable to respect local regulations, especially when cycling within protected natural areas. Stay on marked paths and be mindful of wildlife and the environment.

Q: What are some of the longer, more challenging road cycling routes available?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1070290629/sisapo-from-fuencaliente-circular-by-torrecampo-and-azuel-parque-natural-valle-de-alcudia-y-sierra-madrona" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sisapo from Fuencaliente – circular by Torrecampo and Azuel&lt;/a&gt; cover nearly 128.5 km with significant elevation. Another demanding option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1249475549/puerto-madrona-y-puerto-de-los-rehoyos-circular-por-el-pantano-del-montoro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto Madrona y Puerto de los Rehoyos — circular por el Pantano del Montoro&lt;/a&gt;, a 110 km route with over 2,300 meters of elevation gain.

Q: Can I find places to eat or stay along the road cycling routes?
A: The region includes eight local municipalities, such as Almodóvar del Campo and Fuencaliente, which are likely to offer amenities like cafes, restaurants, and accommodation. While specific roadside cafes might vary, planning your route through these towns will provide opportunities for refreshments and overnight stays.

Q: What makes the night sky special in Valle de Alcudia y Sierra Madrona?
A: Eight local municipalities in the region, including Almodóvar del Campo and Fuencaliente, have joined the Starlight initiative. This recognition highlights the area's exceptionally pristine night skies, making it an excellent destination for stargazing, especially after a day of cycling.
tips: Nice climb in Sierra Madrona along the N-420 road, reaching the pass at 900 meters above sea level. Great views of the surrounding landscape.

On the CM-4202 road, in the Alcudia Valley, you reach the La Bienvenida de Almodóvar del Campo site and the ruins of the ancient city of Sisapo. Roman settlement that exploited lead and silver from the region.
A quiet town that was once a communication hub due to its geographical position - close to the main historical steps that connect the center and the south of the Iberian Peninsula.
